sentences.text: That is correct.
We stomped on that picture, and stomped on that, and tried to get all of our hatred out.
And tried to break Hitler up into little, little pieces, of course.
The hatred never came-- came out.
And we just-- it released a little bit.
But there was so much there, three years of hatred in such little hearts.
How do you get rid of that?
Never.
I remember my brother and I would crawl around when-- when we could get out of bed, to look for stuff to eat.
Like, if we would find a piece of paper, we'd eat that.
[PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] I can't-- can't really recall anything offhand.
My-- my mother, in the beginning of the war, when her mother was taken, had a stroke.
And the whole side of her face was affected.
And in the camps, somebody came who had heard that her brother was shot, quote, 'trying to escape.' But she knew her brother was a little yeshiva student who was very meek, and quiet, and skinny.
And he wouldn't-- he would-- he wouldn't date do anything like that.
So he was just shot outright.
So when she heard that, she had her second stroke.
But she didn't know that anything happened from that second stroke, because we had no mirrors there.
And when my father came to visit her that night, he said, [NON-ENGLISH], he says, your-- your face is normal.
She touched-- this is-- she didn't know what happened.
She'd had that second stroke from hearing about the brother, put everything right again.
Once there was a-- a soldier standing and eating a sandwich by the fence.
And my mother kind of looked at him, and-- and looked at him, and looked at that sandwich, and looked at him.
Looked at that sandwich, and looked at him.
And she was dreaming and drooling over that sandwich.
Finally, he couldn't take it anymore.
He took a look around to see if anybody was there, and he gave her the sandwich, and he walked away.
It was the only time she saw any kindness out of any German.
She had a-- a brother-in-law also, Mr Green.
And he was married to my mother's sister, Tante Rosie.
And they had five kids.
And the Nazis came to him to torture him and say, we can either take you or your wife and kids.
So he broke down, and he said, take my wife and kids.
So they all went to the gas chambers in Auschwitz.
And he survived the war.
He had TB after the war, and he died.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my father's sister survived the war, my Aunt Laney.
She was a nurse in Holland.
And some Dutch people hit her, and another man.
But one day, the Nazis came to inspect the house to see if anybody was there.
Because they-- again they must have heard rumors that they were hiding people there, or that maybe they were buying extra food.
If you were buying extra food in the grocery stores, then somebody would tell on you-- where is this food going?
Well, the other man that was being hidden was dead already, and they didn't know what to do with the body.
But he had just died, so it didn't stink too bad.
And they didn't know what to do.
They saw the Nazis coming, so they quickly rolled up my aunt and this dead body.
And they rolled them up in the carpets, and stood one over there, and one over there.
And the Nazis came and inspected the house, and saw nobody, and they left.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Also, my-- before we went into the camps, in-- in Belgium, there was a yeshiva where my uncles went.
And my grandfather there was very well-known-- very well-known in the community, and very respected by the police.
And the whole yeshiva was taken in and put in jail, because the boys were not allowed to learn.
So they begged my grandfather to go talk to the police and see if he couldn't convince them to let the boys go.
He couldn't do a thing.
All the boys were gassed, including his own two sons.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
And after the war, we had this friend of my parents who came to eat by us every Saturday.
But when he was in the war, he was 17 years old.
And his job was to open and close the gas chambers.
And he saw his mother and sister go in.
And what else? 59 members of my immediate family on both sides died.
We're a very small family that's left.
And we're trying to grow again.
Is there anything else from the war or after that you remember, your thoughts?
I remember in camp, dreaming about if only, if only, we could have just a little bit of [? flour, ?] even just plain bread, with a little butter on it.
Just-- and we would be the happiest people in the world.
That's all I wanted.
After the war, I had nightmares of Nazis chasing me.
And they were always like, this close, you know.
And no matter how fast I was running, they were always right behind him.
And those dreams stopped the minute I married my husband.
It's amazing.
Yes.
On the same street.
On the same side.
We got nothing.
Nothing.
Nothing at all.
I do have photographs that a cousin of mine had in-- he lives in the Jerusalem, in Israel.
And another cousin, also living in Jerusalem.
She gave me from my father's side.
She's my-- a cousin on my father's side.
And I have no idea how they had these photos.
Maybe my uncle had it when he was hiding in the south of France.
I don't know.
After the war?
Pretty much so.
I went to school.
I went to so many schools.
I kept switching schools.
I don't know why.
I-- I think it was because we were moving from one neighborhood to another.
In Den Haag.
And then we moved to Toronto.
Yes.
We were-- now that I remember.
We went to regular school.
But we didn't have school Wednesday afternoons.
Everybody had off.
So Wednesday afternoon, we had to go to Hebrew school.
And Sunday morning, we had to go to Hebrew school.
Saturday was a problem, because we kept the Sabbath.
And everybody else had to be in school.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was a big problem for us.
And there was still a lot of anti-Semitism after the war.
Yeah.
With-- I didn't, but my brother would get a-- a kick in the rear from the gentile boys down the street.
We were called names.
My-- my father got gift of a loan from my grandfather in New York.
And my mother's brother that was in hiding in the south of France, he was a diamond dealer after the war.
So they pooled their money together to put my father up in business to open up a butcher store in Den Haag.
We were the only kosher butcher store in Den Haag and [NON-ENGLISH], and all its surroundings.
It was called the Tel Aviv Butchery.
Yes, it did.
Before.
And then almost nobody came back.
Very few came back afterwards.
I joined a Bnei Akiva youth movement.
It's a-- it was a youth movement to teach you about Israel in the hopes that you would move to Israel.
It's a worldwide organization.
When I joined it, I was seven years old.
And I started right away in the dance group.
And I haven't stopped dancing since then.
Israeli folk dancing, which I teach here now in Oak Park at the Jewish Center.
Yes, my father, two years after the war was over, begged my mother to have another baby.
And she became pregnant and had a little boy.
And when he was six weeks old, I remember I went to Bnei Akiva with my brother, and it was a Saturday.
And I came.
And I looked through the butcher store windows.
We were in a corner, so everything was glass, you could see.
And I saw my mother on the telephone.
So I started to cry before I got in the house, because I knew you can't use the phone on the Sabbath, unless it's a matter of life and death.
So I knew it had to be either my father or my baby brother.
So I came in, and I remember, the baby was in the crib, and my father was lying on the bed.
And my father had said, after lunch, wake me up to go to afternoon prayers, to go to [INAUDIBLE].
She couldn't wake him up.
Just never woke up.
By the time the doctor got there, it was too late.
When was this?
This was in June.
I think he died on the 20th of 1947.
I was eight years old, and my brother was six.
Yes.
I remember my mother was crying and being held up by a lot of women.
And-- and we walked.
We didn't have cars.
There was the hearse.
And then there were a lot of people.
And we were at the end of-- I think-- well, scratch that.
I don't know if we were at the front or the end.
But we were walking down behind the hearse with a lot of people to the funeral.
And I remember that the men were digging him in, and my little brother, six years old, had to pick up the shovel and dig a little bit, and-- and say Kaddish.
About the funeral?
No.
My uncle, the diamond dealer, had, after the war, moved to Israel.
And he-- we wanted to make Aliyah.
We wanted to go to Israel.
And he begged my mother, do not bring the children here.
It is damp and cold in the winter.
There is no meat to build them up.
Take them to the United States or to Canada.
Well, the United States wouldn't let us in.
There had-- there was a quota.
And Canada did let us in.
So my mother ended up working in a tie factory.
And we went to school.
I was 12.
I remember having my bat mitzvah on Sunday in Den Haag.
And the following Friday, we went to Le Havre in-- in France to get on the boat.
And I remember telling my mother, but we're not allowed to ride on the Sabbath.
And she says, we're allowed to, because we're boarding before Sabbath, and we're getting off after.
And that's going to be your home for two weeks.
Then we arrived here, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 14--
--14 Days-- yeah. 14 days later.
We arrived in Halifax.
And from there, we took a three-day train trip-- a sleeper-- to Toronto.
Because we had someone-- someone to speak for us there.
My mother's brother-in-law's sister lived in Toronto.
And she was able to, you know, vouch for us that she would make sure we wouldn't be on welfare.
And she would take care of us.
She found us a place to live, above a Russian or Polish lady.
And I remember coming up the stairs one day, and I heard my mother say to the landlady, don't worry.
She's going to live in New York with her grandfather.
That's all I had to hear.
I said, we just got out of the camps.
And now, she's sending me away to live with somebody else.
I was crying.
And she came after me, and she whispered in my ear, it's only a fake story, because they didn't want any children in this apartment, and we have nowhere to go.
We're not going anywhere.
Not really well.
He-- he would come to visit sometimes, but I really didn't know him that well.
I made friends pretty easily.
No.
Because you can talk a lot with your eyes, and with your hands, and smiling.
And in the beginning, when we arrived, it was summertime, so there was no school.
So we played with the kids on the street, hide and seek.
And-- and we would pick up words from them, like baum.
You know, baum?
Of course, in Dutch, it's a tree-- I mean, in English, it's a tree.
But we didn't know, so we went one, two, three baum.
If you touched the tree, then you know, you could come and look to see where you're hiding.
So eventually, we learned the right words to stuff, and we learned it.
It did put me a year behind in school, because I couldn't read.
So I had to do first grade twice.
In Toronto, yes.
I went to Sunday school.
My brother went to a Hebrew day school.
I wanted to go to public school.
Both brothers went to Hebrew school.
Then, when we got to Toronto after the war, my brother was four years old.
And I remember he came home singing, 'Yes, Jesus loves me.' [LAUGHS]
So I looked at my mother, what school are you sending him to?
He needs to learn his Jewish heritage.
Hitler just tried to destroy us all, and you're sending him to a school.
She says, that was the only place that would take him in where we didn't have to pay.
We didn't have any money.
How long were you in Canada?
I was in Canada from the time I arrived, age 12, til I married my husband at the age of 19.
I met him-- he came to Toronto.
There was a wedding of a friend of his.
And he happened to stay at my boyfriend's house.
And that's how we met.
My boyfriend's house.
My husband's name is Meyer Segal, M-E-Y-E-R, S-E-G-A-L.
And he's a history teacher at Redford High School in Detroit.
And he's a Detroit-born.
We were married on February 15, 1959.
Cold winter day.
Yeah.
We came here.
Our honeymoon was setting up our kitchen on-- I think it was Wildemere in Detroit.
That's right.
I have three boys.
The oldest is 36, Yitzchak Segal.
Y-I-T-Z-C-H-A-K.
And he's married to Linda.
And they have five children.
He's got five children.
And he's a computer analyst.
Remy. ?] He's 14.
[INAUDIBLE] 13.
Doby ?] is 11.
Yehuda is 10.
And [?
Chiffa ?] is eight.
Now, [?
Chiffa ?] is unusual, because she was born-- the Segal families have had no girls in their family for at least 100 years.
And finally, she was born.
I have one son that's in-- does animation voices in Los Angeles, [?
Andy ?] Segal.
And then I have a son in-- in Manhattan.
He is an actuary.
He's the youngest one.
He's 30.
And the one in California is 35.
Since I've been in the States, besides teaching Israeli folk dancing, I was a medical receptionist.
Yes, I do.
At the Jewish Center in Oak Park, Michigan.
And also in my basement.
I like to knit, crochet.
I love to bake and cook.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's it.
Travel.
We love travel.
Both my husband and I love it.
My-- I'm the oldest, but the older brother, Rabbi van Leeuwen lives in Kiryat Arba, which is near Hebron in Israel.
And he has seven children, and two daughters in law, and two grandchildren.
And then, I-- my baby brother, the one that was born six weeks after-- six weeks after-- before my father died, he lives right outside of Tel Aviv with his wife and three kids.
When we can.
We try to rack up free mileage when we go.
My mother, about 13 years ago-- she was 69 years old-- she came down with Alzheimer's disease.
She had it for 10 years.
The last six years of those 10, she was comatose in the hospital.
And three years ago, she passed away in Toronto.
And at the time, I was in Israel for my nephew's wedding.
All that time, and I couldn't even be there.
She remarried three years after we came to Toronto.
She went to this Israel club, and this guy took her home.
And they weren't for each other.
And he says, I want you to come back.
I have this guy I want you to meet.
So she came back, and my stepfather, Sam Miller, was there.
And he took her home.
And he says that's it.
He says, I don't want you to date anybody else.
And they were married for 30 years.
Yes, to Beth Tefilo Emanuel Tikvah on Greenfield.
Orthodox.
Yes, we are.
Not that I can think of.
Because of this treaty that Hitler had with England and Switzerland, its firstborn subjects would not be gassed.
And since I was a first-born born in England, I was put in this other side of Bergen-Belsen so I wouldn't be gassed.
And the reason they kept my father, mother, and brother, kept us together as a family unit was so he would have bigger bargaining power to get more soldiers back from England.
Yes.
Drop all the hatred.
Learn to love one another.
What difference does it matter what skin you are, what color, what-- what your religion is?
We're all put here by the same being.
God put us all on this Earth, in the same shape, in the same form, the same way.
We think the same.
We act the same.
We should act the same.
We should think the same.
I can't say I could ever love the Germans, or anybody that I-- I will even go further, then.
Even though I tell everybody not to hate, I can't get rid of the hatred for the Germans.
I just can't.
And I-- I lash out at it in my way, not by if I see a German, I will spit at you, or kick you, or scream at you.
I just won't buy German products.
Anything that's made in Germany, goes-- if I [INAUDIBLE], inadvertently buy it, and I see it on the bottom of something, take it right back to the store.
I don't think so.
I think it's a wonderful thing that you are doing, volunteering for this.
And I thank Steven Spielberg for undertaking this wonderful project.
Needs to be told.
And I think every student in the world should be made aware of these tapes.
Thank you.
